claim: NM weighted average price per gram of cannabis pre-rolls fell 48.17%, from $10.38 to $5.38, between April 2022 and February 2025
context: Similarly, the weighted average price per gram of cannabis flower pre-rolls is declining. In April 2022, the weighted average price per gram of a cannabis flower pre-roll was $10.38 a gram, before sales tax or excise tax. In February of 2025, the weighted average price per gram of cannabis flower pre-roll was $5.38, before sales tax or excise tax. This is up from a low of $5.24 in August of 2024. From the first month to the most recent month in the provided BioTrack dataset, there is a 48.17% decline in the weighted average price per gram of cannabis flower pre-rolls.
notes: Prices before sales or excise tax; low of $5.24 reached in August 2024

claim: Cannabis flower and pre-rolls accounted for 58.36% of total all-time NM cannabis sales in dollars
context: Cannabis flower and uninfused cannabis flower pre-rolls accounted for 58.36% of total all-time sales in dollars. Cannabis concentrates and cannabis vapes accounted for 26.65% of total all-time sales in dollars. In total, the four cannabis product groups accounted for 85.01% of total sales in dollars.

claim: Cannabis concentrates and vapes accounted for 26.65% of total all-time NM cannabis sales in dollars
context: Cannabis concentrates and cannabis vapes accounted for 26.65% of total all-time sales in dollars. In total, the four cannabis product groups accounted for 85.01% of total sales in dollars.

claim: NM flower and pre-roll market share declined from 63.92% in April 2022 to 52.81% in February 2025
context: Overall demand for cannabis flower and uninfused cannabis flower pre-rolls, as a percentage of total sales in dollars, has declined from 63.92% in April of 2022 to 52.81% in February of 2025.
notes: Decline of over 11 percentage points

claim: NM concentrates and vapes market share rose from 25.19% in April 2022 to 29.66% in February 2025
context: Cannabis concentrates and cannabis vapes, as a percentage of total sales in dollars, has risen from 25.19% in April of 2022 to 29.66% in February of 2025. Cannabis infused with concentrates, as a percentage of total sales in dollars, has risen from 0.19% in April of 2022 to 5.07% in February of 2025.

claim: NM concentrate-infused flower market share rose from 0.19% in April 2022 to 5.07% in February 2025
context: Cannabis flower products infused with concentrates rose from 0.19% of total sales in dollars in April of 2022 to 5.07% of total sales in dollars in February of 2025.

claim: Non-patients source 61.10% of cannabis expenditures from the regulated market in NM; 88.9% from regulated and gray markets combined
context: Non-patients source 88.9% of their cannabis expenditures from either the regulated or gray markets, with 61.10% coming from the regulated market alone.
notes: Survey-based finding from RDCOS; n=198 past-month consumers

claim: NM medical cannabis patients spend an average of $310.44 per month vs. $205.79 for non-medical consumers
context: Medical cannabis patients consume higher quantities of cannabis across all product types and spend significantly more per month ($310.44) than non-medical consumers ($205.79).
notes: Survey-based finding; patients spend ~51% more per month than non-patients

claim: NM non-medical cannabis consumers spend an average of $205.79 per month on cannabis products
context: Medical cannabis patients consume more cannabis products on average and spend more dollars per month, for an average of $310.44, whereas non-medical cannabis consumers spend $205.79 on cannabis products per month. This finding is consistent across most states with both medical and adult-use cannabis markets.

claim: NM medical patients source 57.37% of cannabis expenditures from the regulated market; 83.71% from regulated and gray markets combined
context: Medical cannabis patients have a slightly lower percentage, with only 83.71% of all dollars spent on cannabis products being sourced from the legal/regulated cannabis market or the gray market. For medical cannabis patients, a slightly lower percentage of 57.37% of all dollars spent on cannabis products is sourced from the regulated cannabis market, exclusively.
notes: Lower regulated market capture for patients than non-patients possibly due to higher consumption volumes

claim: NM cannabis product category market share breakdown across all-time sales (April 2022 - February 2025)
context: Table 3. New Mexico Product Categories and All Time % of Sales. Broad Cannabis Product Categories and their BioTrack Cannabis Categories in New Mexico with All Time % of Sales from April 2022 to February 2025.

table_data.table_title: New Mexico Product Categories and All Time % of Sales

notes: Data from BioTrack seed-to-sale system

claim: NM past-month consumer average units and expenditures by market source and patient status
context: Table 1. Past-Month New Mexico Cannabis Consumer Average Units Across All Market Sources. Data Source: RDCOS. Table 2. Total Past-Month New Mexico Consumer Averages Expenditures Across All Market. Data Source: RDCOS.

table_data.table_title: Past-Month NM Cannabis Consumer Average Expenditures Across All Market Sources

notes: Survey-based data from RDCOS; n=198 past-month consumers; data collected no later than January 19, 2025

claim: NM cannabis vapes reached peak weighted average price per gram of $76.94 in May 2022
context: In May 2022, cannabis concentrates and vapes constituted 24.08% of total sales in dollars, the minimum over our observed period. That month, cannabis vapes experienced the height of their weighted average price per gram at $76.94. Nine months later, in January of 2023, cannabis concentrates would reach their maximum weighted average price per gram at $61.84. Both peaks were likely driven by excessive demand and a lack of capacity to quickly replenish supply in the early months of cannabis legalization in New Mexico.
notes: Price before sales or excise tax

claim: NM cannabis concentrates reached peak weighted average price per gram of $61.84 in January 2023
context: Nine months later, in January of 2023, cannabis concentrates would reach their maximum weighted average price per gram at $61.84. Both peaks were likely driven by excessive demand and a lack of capacity to quickly replenish supply in the early months of cannabis legalization in New Mexico.
notes: Price before sales or excise tax

claim: NM cannabis cured pounds produced Oct-Nov grew from 74,231 lbs (2022) to 148,968 lbs (2023) to 188,482 lbs (2024)
context: Table 5. New Mexico Cannabis Market Cannabis for Oct-Nov in LBS.

table_data.table_title: New Mexico Cannabis Market Cannabis for Oct-Nov in LBS

notes: Other pounds are increasing at a higher rate than cured pounds, likely due to increased demand for concentrates and vapes

claim: NM cultivators may grow up to 20,000 mature cannabis plants but most are operating well below the limit; limited justification for raising cap
context: Cultivators may grow up to 20,000 mature cannabis plants at one time, but our analysis indicates that the average number of plants grown per licensee falls below this threshold. However, it is worth noting that the BioTrack data does not differentiate between the stages in which plants are grown. That is, there is no way to know what percentage of plants are immature or mature in the current data set. That being said, the vast majority of cultivators are still not reaching the 20,000 max in totality (i.e., across what is assumed immature and mature).
notes: Per section 26-2C-40 NMSA 1978; CPPC recommends against raising the cap

claim: NM regulated adult-use cannabis market is likely headed toward a market correction with licensing consolidation across all areas of the supply chain
context: Based on the time-trend analysis of track-and-trace data and supported by the HHI and demand analysis, the regulated adult-use cannabis market is likely headed toward a market correction wherein licensing consolidation will occur in all areas of the supply chain. The degree of this will be dependent on the capture of the regulated sales compared to illicit capture.

claim: NM legalized cannabis for adults 21+ in April 2021 with first regulated retail sales beginning April 2022
context: New Mexico legalized cannabis for adults aged 21 and older in April 2021, with the first regulated retail sales beginning in April 2022. The state's regulatory model, marked by low barriers to entry for businesses and uncapped licenses, has fostered a competitive environment for operators and low prices for consumers.
notes: Uncapped licensing model with low barriers to entry

claim: A license moratorium could temporarily stabilize NM market but would not prevent consolidation and could drive prices up for consumers
context: A license moratorium could temporarily stabilize New Mexico's competitive cannabis market by halting new entrants and providing relief to existing operators. However, it would not prevent consolidation, could further entrench existing dominant players, reduce consumer choice, and ultimately drive prices up for consumers. Allowing the market to self-correct would preserve competitiveness and encourage stabilization over time. Yet, without intervention, small businesses remain especially vulnerable to exiting the market or consolidation, potentially undermining goals of broad market participation by small and diverse businesses.
notes: Policy consideration from CPPC; not a regulatory decision

claim: NM cannabis edibles accounted for 10.15% of total all-time sales in dollars
context: Cannabis Edibles accounted for 10.15% of All Time % of Sales from April 2022 to February 2025.

claim: Approximately 21-30% of cannabis sales in Nevada occur through unlicensed transactions
context: Eliminating the Unlicensed Market: Nevada's unlicensed cannabis market is multi-faceted and includes both large-scale traffickers as well as smaller operators and individuals. Approximately 21-30% of cannabis sales in Nevada occur through unlicensed transactions, posing safety issues for the public and tourists and harming the state's efforts to create a healthy, controlled marketplace.
notes: Range of 21-30% cited; sourced from a 2024 report commissioned by the State of Nevada (TPMA)

claim: Nevada's unlicensed cannabis market represents approximately $242 million to $370 million in sales upon which state tax is not being collected
context: Within Nevada, a considerable segment of cannabis sales occurs outside the regulated framework. A 2024 report, commissioned by the State of Nevada, estimates that 21-30% of cannabis sales are through unlicensed transactions—approximately $242 million to $370 million in sales upon which state tax is not being collected.
notes: Range estimate; sourced from Nevada Cannabis Compliance Board Cannabis Market Study prepared by TPMA (Aug. 2024)

claim: Nevada has the second largest illegal cannabis market per capita after California when comparing four western states
context: In addition, Nevada has the second largest illegal market per capita, after California, when comparing four western states.
notes: Comparison limited to four western states; sourced from TPMA Cannabis Market Study

claim: Washington State reports 77% of consumers obtain their cannabis from a licensed store, higher than the 57% average among all legal states
context: No state or jurisdiction has successfully eliminated unlicensed sources for cannabis, but many are having success speeding the transition and reducing public impacts. For example, Washington State reports 77% of consumers obtain their cannabis from a licensed store, which is higher than the 57% average among all legal states.
notes: Sourced from International Cannabis Policy Study, University of Waterloo (Sept. 2022)

claim: Average among all legal states for consumers obtaining cannabis from a licensed store is 57%
context: No state or jurisdiction has successfully eliminated unlicensed sources for cannabis, but many are having success speeding the transition and reducing public impacts. For example, Washington State reports 77% of consumers obtain their cannabis from a licensed store, which is higher than the 57% average among all legal states.
notes: Sourced from International Cannabis Policy Study, University of Waterloo (Sept. 2022)

claim: Canada reports as many as 70% of consumers are purchasing cannabis from legal sources
context: Canada has similarly reported as many as 70% of consumers are purchasing from legal sources with observable declines in illegal cannabis storefronts across its municipalities.
notes: Canada data, included for comparison; sourced from Health Canada (Oct. 2022)

claim: Las Vegas metropolitan area is visited by more than 40 million tourists each year
context: The Las Vegas metropolitan area is visited by more than 40 million tourists each year, most of whom are unaware of the state's cannabis purchase and consumption laws.
notes: Sourced from Las Vegas Convention and Visitors Authority 2023 Visitor Profile Study

claim: 70% of overnight guests in Las Vegas stay in the Strip Resort Corridor where legal cannabis operators are restricted from operating or delivering
context: Legal operators are restricted from operating in or delivering to the Strip Resort Corridor, where 70% of overnight guests stay.
notes: Sourced from Las Vegas Convention and Visitors Authority 2023 Visitor Profile Study

claim: Licensed cannabis stores in Nevada must be set back 1,500 feet from gaming establishments
context: Nevada legalized cannabis for adult-use in 2017, but restrictions in state law limit tourist access to regulated cannabis. Licensed stores must be set back 1,500 feet from gaming establishments, preventing any from opening on the Las Vegas Strip.
notes: Codified in NRS 678B.210(3)(a)(2)(II) and NRS 678B.250(3)(a)(2)(II)

claim: At least four murders associated with unlicensed cannabis sales occurred in Las Vegas over the last year
context: The unlicensed cannabis market is a public safety issue. Unlicensed sale of drugs, including cannabis, can lead to violence, resulting in injury or death. At least four murders associated with unlicensed sales have occurred in Las Vegas over the last year alone.
notes: Based on multiple media reports cited in footnotes 31-34

claim: Nevada derives 43% of its gross domestic product from tourism
context: For a state that derives 43% of its gross domestic product from tourism, safety risks for tourists could pose risks to the state's economy should a tourist be seriously hurt or killed.
notes: Sourced from Nevada Resort Association (Apr. 2023)

claim: Cannabis businesses pay an effective federal tax rate of up to 70% due to IRS Code 280E
context: IRC section 280E prohibits businesses that produce, transport or sell Schedule I drugs from deducting standard business expenses on their taxes. There are no exceptions for cannabis businesses legalized under state law. Because they cannot take standard business deductions, cannabis businesses pay an effective federal tax rate of up 70 percent. This creates a tremendous financial burden, particularly for small and equity operators.

claim: As of October 2024, 38 states allow medical cannabis and 24 states plus two US territories and DC have legalized recreational use
context: As of October 2024, 38 states allow medical cannabis and 24 states, two U.S. territories, and the District of Columbia have legalized recreational use.
notes: Sourced from National Conference of State Legislatures (June 2024)

claim: Public opinion on cannabis legalization shifted from 31% support in 2000 to 88% support for medical or recreational use in 2024
context: 2000: 31% believed that cannabis should be legalized. 2024: 88% now believe it should be legal for medical or recreational use.
notes: Sourced from Pew Research Center (Mar. 2024)

claim: 22% of Americans have chosen a travel destination based on whether adult-use cannabis is allowed
context: A 2022 Harris Poll found that: 22% of Americans have chosen a travel destination based whether adult-use cannabis is allowed.
notes: Sourced from 2022 Harris Poll

claim: 30% of American adults 21+ consider legal status of adult-use cannabis an important factor when choosing a vacation destination; 50% of millennials within this group
context: 30% of American adults 21 and older consider legal status of adult-use cannabis an important factor when choosing a vacation destination. Within this group, 50% of millennials consider cannabis an important factor.
notes: Sourced from 2022 Harris Poll

claim: More than two-thirds of people who use cannabis report cutting back on drinking
context: More than two-thirds of people who use cannabis report cutting back on drinking. More Americans now use cannabis on a daily or near daily frequency than alcohol.
notes: Sourced from TD Cowen report 'Cannabis Beats Booze' (Jan. 2024)

claim: More Americans now use cannabis on a daily or near daily frequency than alcohol
context: More Americans now use cannabis on a daily or near daily frequency than alcohol.
notes: Sourced from Caulkins (2024), Addiction journal, covering self-reported data from 1979-2022

claim: Nevada charges a Retail Cannabis Tax of 10% and a Wholesale Cannabis Tax of 15%
context: Nevada currently charges two cannabis-specific taxes: A Retail Cannabis Tax of 10% of the sale price, charged at the time of sale. A Wholesale Cannabis Tax of 15% of the fair market value, charged at the first wholesale sale.

claim: 50% of states that have legalized cannabis have adopted measures allocating funding for research
context: Some states have invested in cannabis research both to inform policy development and in response to this funding need. Fifty percent of states that have legalized cannabis have adopted measures allocating funding for research.
notes: Sourced from Balla, Boyle, and Dempsey (Aug. 2024) manuscript

claim: Arizona allocates up to $25 million in clinical trial cannabis research grants distributed over five years
context: Arizona: Up to $25 million in clinical trial research grants distributed over five years.
notes: From Arizona Proposition 207 (2020)

claim: Illinois allocates 2% of cannabis tax revenues for research-related activities including a Cannabis Research Institute
context: Illinois: 2% of cannabis tax revenues for research-related activities, including to fund a Cannabis Research Institute.
notes: From Illinois Cannabis Regulation and Tax Act (2019)

claim: Minnesota allocates $2.5 million annually to the University of Minnesota to fund a Center for Cannabis Research
context: Minnesota: $2.5 million annually to the University of Minnesota to fund a Center for Cannabis Research.
notes: From Minnesota H.F. 100 (2023)

claim: This report was informed by conversations with more than 60 stakeholders conducted over three months
context: This policy report was informed by conversations with more than 60 stakeholders conducted over three months, from August until October 2024.

claim: Nevada's cannabis consumption lounges first opened in early 2024, with several owned by social equity businesses
context: Nevada adopted laws authorizing cannabis consumption lounges in 2021. After a long and robust public process to develop rules, and for licensees to build out compliant locations, the first lounges opened in early 2024. Several are owned by social equity businesses.

claim: Oregon, California, and Washington have adopted laws authorizing interstate cannabis trade between consenting legalized states
context: State legislatures in Oregon (2019), California (2022) and Washington (2023) have adopted nearly identical laws authorizing trade between consenting legalized states. Under these frameworks, states would enter into agreements with each other authorizing distribution across state borders. None of these three states have entered into an interstate agreement with another state yet.
notes: None of these states had entered into an agreement yet as of December 2024

claim: Nevada retail prices for flower fell about 10% and edibles fell about 10% over 2023; concentrates fell about 4% and pre-rolls fell about 1%
context: Over the course of 2023, Nevada retail prices for flower and edibles fell by about 10%, concentrates fell by about 4%, and pre-rolls fell by about 1%.
notes: Based on Metrc track-and-trace data

claim: Nevada flower retail prices in April and May 2024 were down more than 21% year-over-year vs same months in 2023
context: As shown in Table 1, over the first five months of 2024, the decrease in flower prices accelerated such that prices in April and May 2024 were down more than 21% year-over-year (vs. April and May 2023).
notes: Based on Metrc track-and-trace data

claim: One-year retail price changes January-May 2024 vs same months in 2023 across product categories
context: Table 1. One-year retail price changes, January–May 2024. Difference between each month's average retail price and the retail price on the same month in 2023.
notes: Source: Author calculations using anonymized data from state Metrc track-and-trace system

claim: Ordinary flower retail prices declined from $6.18 per gram in May 2023 to $5.11 per gram in May 2024, a decline of about 17%
context: Ordinary flower prices declined from $6.18 per gram in May 2023 to $5.11 per gram in May 2024, a decline of about 17%.
notes: Linear decline of about 9.5 cents/month, R-squared = 0.98

claim: Solvent-based concentrate retail prices decreased about 27% between May 2023 and May 2024
context: Retail prices for solvent-based concentrates, a category that includes the THC distillates that make up most of the vape market, have been falling even more rapidly. Solvent-based retail prices decreased about 27% between May 2023 and May 2024.
notes: Based on Metrc track-and-trace data

claim: Cannabis flower accounts for roughly 45% of the Nevada market by both volume (grams total THC) and revenue (retail dollars)
context: Cannabis flower accounts for roughly 45% of the Nevada market, whether measured by volume (in terms of grams total THC) or revenue (in retail dollars spent).
notes: Based on Metrc track-and-trace data

claim: Pre-rolls have about 8% of Nevada market by volume and 12% by revenue; concentrates about half by volume and one-third by revenue; edibles about 2% by volume and 12% by revenue
context: Figure 5 shows the comparison for pre-rolls (the other dried-flower category), which have about 8% of the market by volume and 12% of the market by revenue. Figure 6 shows the same comparison for concentrates, which have about half the market by volume and about one-third by revenue. Figure 7 shows the comparison for edibles, which have about 2% of the market by volume and 12% by revenue.
notes: Volume measured in grams total THC

claim: Average wholesale flower prices in Nevada averaged about $1,350 per pound and fell to about $1,270 in December 2024
context: Average wholesale flower prices averaged about $1,350 per pound and fell throughout 2023, to about $1,270 in December 2024.
notes: Based on anonymized CCB METRC track-and-trace data

claim: Nevada wholesale prices were more than double California median prices; wholesale unpackaged flower prices declined almost 40% in 2023
context: Nevada's wholesale average was high for the U.S. western states in 2023, more than double California median prices. In 2023, wholesale prices declined most for unpackaged flower (almost 40%). Prices decreased by about 12% for packaged flower, and prices decreased only slightly for pre-rolls.
notes: Based on Metrc track-and-trace data

claim: Nevada wholesale-to-retail markups and average prices by product type, January-May 2024
context: Table 2. One-year retail price changes, January–May 2024. Product Type, Wholesale Price, Retail Price, Retail Markup Pct, Retail Dollar Markup.
notes: Source: Author calculations using anonymized data from state Metrc track-and-trace system

claim: Total monthly retail adult-use revenue for all smokable cannabis flower types has been relatively flat at about $36-40 million, or about 55% of all adult-use retail revenue
context: Figure 17 shows that total monthly retail adult-use revenue for all smokable cannabis flower types (including flower, small buds/popcorn, shake, pre-rolls, and infused pre-rolls) has been relatively flat at about $36–40 million, or about 55% of all adult-use retail revenue.
notes: Based on Metrc track-and-trace data

claim: Total monthly revenue for all cannabis extract products is relatively flat at $28-30 million, or about 45% of all adult-use retail revenue
context: Total monthly revenue for all cannabis extract products (including concentrates, resin/rosin, edibles, tinctures, and oils) is relatively flat at $28–30 million, or about 45% of all adult-use retail revenue. This 55/45 relationship was remarkably stable over 2023 and the first half of 2024.
notes: Based on Metrc track-and-trace data

claim: Retailers report not being able to easily sell cannabis flower with less than 20% THC potency, with increasing customer demand for products above 30% THC
context: Retailers report not being able to easily sell cannabis flower with less than 20% THC potency, and increasingly facing customers who demand products with more than 30% THC.
notes: Based on industry interviews

claim: More than 80% of legal cannabis grown in Nevada is indoor-grown, the most expensive type; Nevada is the only western state to grow most of its cannabis indoors
context: This is partly because more than 80% of legal cannabis grown in Nevada is indoor-grown, the most expensive type. Nevada is the only state in this group to grow most of its cannabis indoors.
notes: Compared to California, Oregon, Washington, and Arizona

claim: Nevada wholesale cannabis prices are about double the prices in four neighboring Western states (CA, OR, WA, AZ all between $700 and $900 per pound)
context: Nevada wholesale prices for legal cannabis are about double the prices in the four neighboring Western states of California, Oregon, Washington, and Arizona (all between $700 and $900 per pound).

claim: Wholesale price per pound of greenhouse-grown cannabis: Arizona $584, California $646, Oregon $588, Washington $777, Nevada $1,192
context: Table 3. Wholesale price per pound of greenhouse-grown cannabis from a selection of wholesalers in Nevada and other Western states, by grow type. Source: Cannabis Benchmarks, trailing average, 4/1/2022–3/31/2023.
notes: Source: Cannabis Benchmarks, trailing average April 2022 - March 2023; greenhouse-grown only

claim: In an interstate market, premium-priced local Nevada cannabis expected to maintain no more than about 10% market share by volume and 15-20% by revenue
context: A small market for premium-priced local cannabis will persist, but like the market for craft beer, we do not expect it to maintain a retail market share of more than about 10% by volume and 15–20% by revenue.
notes: Projection by UC Davis Cannabis Economics Group

claim: The cheapest legal flower in America is priced somewhere in the neighborhood of $100 to $200 per pound, found in Northern California or the Pacific Northwest
context: The cheapest legal flower in America, which is generally found somewhere in Northern California or the Pacific Northwest, is priced somewhere in the neighborhood of $100 to $200 per pound—still perhaps 10 times the wholesale price of weed in Mexico.

claim: THC beverages at Nevada cannabis retailers are typically priced at about $10 per can, double the $5 typically charged at a liquor store in Texas where regulated as hemp
context: THC beverages...are widely available only at cannabis retailers, mostly licensed ones, where they are typically priced at about $10 per can. This is about double the $5 typically charged at a liquor store in Texas, where the product is regulated as hemp and not cannabis.

claim: Legal cannabis licensees in Nevada command 100% share of legal in-state retail sales due to interstate trade restrictions; illegal sellers have 100% market share in areas within 1,500 feet of gaming establishments
context: The illegal market still commands a substantial market share in the state, especially in tourist areas such as the Las Vegas Strip. Because of the law that legal cannabis retailers cannot be located within 1,500 feet of gaming establishments, illegal sellers have a 100% market share in those areas.

claim: Nevada greenhouse wholesale prices were about $1,200 per pound (50-100% higher than other western states) and indoor prices about $1,700 per pound (40-70% higher)
context: Nevada greenhouse prices were about $1,200 per pound (50–100% higher than the other states), and indoor prices were about $1,700 per pound (40–70% higher than the other states).
notes: Sourced from Cannabis Benchmarks, non-random selection of producers

claim: 21 states have adopted restrictions on cartoons or illustrations in cannabis marketing; 20 states restrict depiction of consumption
context: Commonly Prohibited Cannabis Marketing Practices. Number of states that have adopted each type of restriction: Cartoon or bubble-like fonts 2, Images of fruit 4, Colloquial terms or slang 5, Illuminated or neon colors 5, Celebrity references 6, Images of a leaf or a bud 6, Mascots or inflatables 7, Digital pop-up ads 8, Imitation of candy 9, Health claims 9, Toys 14, Depiction of consumption 20, Cartoons or illustrations 21.

table_data.restriction: Images of a leaf or a bud

notes: Based on state laws and regulations as of August 1, 2024

claim: State audience composition requirements for cannabis advertising vary: 9 states require 70% age 21+, 3 states require 71.6%, 5 states require 85%, 2 states require 90%
context: State Audience Composition Requirements. Number of states that have adopted each requirement: 9 states require 70% of audience age 21+, 3 states require 71.6%, 5 states require 85%, 2 states require 90%.
notes: Based on state laws and regulations as of August 1, 2024; alcohol industry standard is 73.8%

claim: Current alcohol advertising guidelines limit advertisements to places where at least 73.8% of the audience is 21 or older
context: One of the most common provisions borrowed from the alcohol industry is the limitation of cannabis advertisements to places where at least a certain percentage of the audience is reasonably expected to be 21 years of age or older. Current alcohol advertising guidelines limit advertisements to places where at least 73.8% of the audience is 21or older.
notes: From DISCUS Code of Responsible Practices (2023)

claim: Every state with legalized and regulated cannabis has enacted restrictions on marketing activities by cannabis companies
context: So, it comes as little surprise that every state with legalized and regulated cannabis has enacted restrictions on marketing activities by cannabis companies. Their approaches vary, however, and even those measures that are commonplace, such as prohibiting cartoons on packaging and limiting advertising to specific audiences, differ from one state to another.

claim: Cannabis marketing featuring illustrations, multiple colors, animals, or action significantly boosts teen interest in cannabis products
context: Findings show that marketing featuring illustrations, multiple colors, animals, or action significantly boosts teen interest in cannabis products. Appeals focusing on positive sensations and psychoactive effects further heighten this interest. Notably, unlike alcohol and e-cigarette ads, cannabis ads that include product descriptions and composition details also tend to heighten teen interest, likely due to the novelty and unfamiliarity of regulated cannabis products.
notes: Sourced from Padon et al. (2024) working paper, Prevention Policy Group, Public Health Institute

claim: California has the lowest wholesale cannabis flower prices at $150 per pound; Illinois is highest at $1,555 per pound (over 10x higher)
context: To avoid rapid obsolescence, I avoid stating average prices in dollars, which are frequently changing. Instead, I state the results in relative terms. In these data, California has the lowest wholesale prices, at $150 per pound. Illinois' lowest price in the same time period, by comparison, is $1,555—more than 10 times higher.
notes: Source: Cannabis Benchmarks, January 2025

claim: Cheapest wholesale flower prices by state relative to California (January 2025): 15-state ranking with year-over-year and 3-year price changes
context: Table 2. Which US states have the cheapest low-end wholesale weed? Differences between 15 states in lowest reported wholesale price for 1 lb legal cannabis flower, January 2025.
notes: Source: Cannabis Benchmarks, January 2025. Not a random sample; estimates likely biased upward.

claim: Cheapest retail weed by state: Oregon is cheapest, Nevada is 13th with +88% price premium (December 2022 data)
context: Table 3. US states with the cheapest retail weed. Differences between states in retail price of 1/8 ounce cannabis flower.
notes: Source: UC Davis regression analysis of Weedmaps retail price data (Dec 2022, 28 states, 1.52 million observations). Oregon base price about $22 per 1/8 oz.

claim: As of January 2025, low-end wholesale outdoor prices were below $300 in four states: California, Michigan, Washington, and Oregon
context: As of January 2025, low-end wholesale outdoor prices were below $300 in four states: California, Michigan, Washington, and Oregon. Oregon had America's cheapest low-end greenhouse-grown weed, below $300 per pound. Michigan had the cheapest low-end indoor-grown weed, at $350 per pound.
notes: Source: Cannabis Benchmarks, January 2025

claim: Michigan has the cheapest low-end indoor-grown weed at $350 per pound (January 2025)
context: Michigan had the cheapest low-end indoor-grown weed, at $350 per pound.
notes: Source: Cannabis Benchmarks, January 2025

claim: Wholesale outdoor prices in California, Colorado, Oregon, and Washington converged and stabilized around $400-$450 per pound since 2022
context: It is interesting to note the convergence between states over time, the common rapid decline, and the relative stability since 2022, when prices settled around a low common range (about $400–$450 per pound).
notes: Source: Cannabis Benchmarks, April 2024. Average outdoor prices (not low transaction prices).

claim: Nevada ranked 5th in interstate weed economic potential with population of 3.2 million; wholesale greenhouse prices fell 50% in 2024
context: Nevada is the least populous state in the top 10 but has exciting potential as an interstate cannabis exporter, beginning with a business-friendly environment and very low prices for electricity, land, and labor. Wholesale greenhouse prices, after stagnating for two years, fell 50% in 2024—a good sign for long-term competitiveness. Las Vegas also has a big opportunity in weed tourism, especially if regulatory changes can unlock new potential for on-premise retail.
notes: Rankings by Robin Goldstein of UC Davis; subjective ranking based on per-capita economic potential

claim: Nevada wholesale greenhouse cannabis prices fell 50% in 2024
context: Wholesale greenhouse prices, after stagnating for two years, fell 50% in 2024—a good sign for long-term competitiveness.
notes: From Robin's Weed Rankings; Nevada ranked 5th overall

claim: Nevada wholesale price premium of +200% over California's lowest wholesale price as of January 2025; with 1-year price decline of -49%
context: Table 2: Nevada +200% state price premium, Jan 2025. 1-year change in lowest price: -49%. 3-year change per year in lowest price: -17%.
notes: Source: Cannabis Benchmarks, January 2025

claim: Michigan's lowest wholesale prices fell 63% year-over-year as of January 2025, the fastest decline among 15 tracked states
context: Table 2: Michigan +20% price premium vs California, 1-year change in lowest price: -63%, 3-year change: -45%.
notes: Source: Cannabis Benchmarks, January 2025

claim: Michigan has annual retail cannabis sales of $350 per state resident, cited as evidence that low prices are the key to a big legal industry
context: Mature, fiercely competitive businesses are cranking out high volume to satisfy substantial local demand, and legal retail is raking in an impressive $350 in annual retail sales per resident—more evidence that low prices are the key to a big legal industry.
notes: Michigan data; population 10 million

claim: Missouri tracking $1.4 billion in annual sales ($226 per state resident) with tax collections over $200 million
context: A relative newcomer to legalization, Missouri only opened its recreational system in 2023 but is now tracking $1.4B in annual sales ($226 per state resident), with tax collections over $200M.
notes: Missouri data; population 6 million

claim: 30-state survey of relative cannabis input costs comparing regulatory burden, labor, electricity, land, and gas costs
context: Table 5. 30-state survey: How will cannabis costs vary by state in the future? Relative costs of legal cannabis inputs.
notes: Partial extract of 30-state table. Sources: UC Davis estimates (regulatory); BLS May 2023 (labor); EIA Oct 2024 (electricity); USDA Aug 2024 (land); AAA Dec 2024 (gas). Nevada shows V. Low labor at $9.64/hr and V. Low electricity.

claim: Top 25 US states ranked by per-capita economic potential for interstate weed market; Oklahoma ranked #1, Oregon #2, California #3, Michigan #4, Nevada #5
context: Robin's Weed Rankings. I rank the top 25 US states by economic potential in a national US market with legal interstate commerce.
notes: Subjective rankings by Robin Goldstein of UC Davis, dated March 5, 2025; ranked by per-capita economic potential across all weed market segments

claim: When legal weed finally knocks most illegal weed out of the market, it will likely be at about $5 per eighth, not the current $40 or even $15
context: The locations most likely to win in a national price competition will be places where weed can be grown and sold most cheaply.
notes: From 2024 Cannabis Economy report page 26; this report references it. Author projection of long-term price equilibrium.

claim: THC hemp market captured a substantial share of legal weed dollars in the US between 2022 and 2024; boom centered in Southeastern states
context: Between 2022 and 2024, the THC hemp market captured a substantial share of legal weed dollars in the US. The boom is still centered around a few Southeastern states, but it's built on a foundation of interstate commerce. In this market, THC flower, vapes, gummies, and beverages are being shipped across the country both wholesale and direct-to-consumer.
notes: Hemp States include FL, GA, KY, LA, NC, SC, TN, TX, and MN among others

claim: Federal regulatory scenarios table showing market outcomes under current Schedule I, Farm Bill hemp ban, rescheduling to Schedule III, descheduling, and federal legalization with regulation
context: Table 1. Current situation and possible future outcomes for legal US interstate commerce under different regulatory scenarios.

scenarios: Farm Bill bans most intoxicating hemp
interstate_commerce: Illegal (hemp tolerated unless Farm Bill bans)
Illegal unless FDA-approved (hemp tolerated unless Farm Bill bans)
Legal subject to federal agency rules/taxes

key_outcomes.schedule_I: Legal and illegal prices both falling; legal licensees struggle to compete on price
key_outcomes.deschedule: Legal market merges with hemp; economic boom; tax collections increase
key_outcomes.federal_legalize: Legal cannabis industry continues to decline; cannabis prices increase; most harm to low-budget consumers

notes: Summary of complex multi-scenario table; full detail in original report

claim: Oregon retail price for 1/8 oz cannabis flower about $22 - cheapest in the US (December 2022)
context: Percent difference between the THC-adjusted retail price in each state vs. the lowest-priced state (Oregon, about $22 per 1/8 oz).
notes: Source: UC Davis regression analysis of Weedmaps retail price data (Dec 2022, 28 states, 1.52 million observations)

claim: A simple ban on out-of-state cannabis imports would be illegal under the Commerce Clause of the US Constitution; states may use environmental, safety, or worker-protection standards as protectionist measures
context: If weed is federally descheduled, states might try to get creative with their regulatory protections. A simple ban on out-of-state imports would be illegal under the Commerce Clause of the US Constitution. Some states have gotten around this, however, by imposing costly environmental, safety, health, or worker-protection standards that out-of-state exporters are unable or unwilling to comply with.

claim: When an Illinois producer at $1,500/lb faces competition from California at $150/lb (30% THC), immediate protectionist lobbying incentives arise
context: The first and biggest effect of interstate commerce will be immediate competition between states on price. The initial juxtaposition of domestic and imported prices might be shocking in some states. When a producer in Illinois, whose legal weed with 25% THC is fetching $1,500 per pound from Illinois retailers in the gated market, faces competition from 30% THC weed from California at $150 per pound, the legal Illinois producer encounters immediate incentives to (try to) protect its business livelihood by lobbying the state to take protectionist measures and prevent cheap out-of-state retail competition.

claim: Vermont total cannabis monthly market size estimated at $18,991,290 (central estimate), with range from $12.1M to $27.4M
context: Total VT Cannabis Monthly Market Size Estimates - All Regulated Cannabis Expenditures. NSDUH 2023 Estimate; CPPC Estimate: $18,991,290. Range spans from $12,063,960 (NSDUH Lower Bound; CPPC Lower Bound) to $27,363,900 (NSDUH Upper Bound; CPPC Upper Bound).

table_data.table_title: Total VT Cannabis Monthly Market Size Estimates

notes: Methodology: Recruited respondents via Cint Online Panels, applied post-hoc raking using BRFSS and NSDUH data, utilized inverse probability weighting. Central estimate implies ~$228M annual market.

claim: Vermont cannabis consumers spend an average of $196.97/month total, with $134.69 at adult-use/medical dispensaries (68.4% in regulated market)
context: VT Cannabis Average Monthly Expenditures by All Past-Month Consumers. Total Spending: $196.97. Adult-Use/Medical Spending: $134.69. Flower: $78.59, Pre-Rolls: $23.02, Edibles: $30.88, Beverages: $3.38, Concentrates: $18.37, Vapes: $41.82, Tinctures: $0.30, Topicals: $0.61, Capsules: $-.

table_data.table_title: Average Monthly Expenditures by All Past-Month Consumers

claim: Vermont medical patients spend $251.73/month on cannabis vs $187.38 for non-patients (34% more)
context: Average Monthly Expenditures by Patient Status. Non-Patient total spending: $187.38, dispensary spending: $128.56. Patient total spending: $251.73, dispensary spending: $169.78.
notes: Patients spend significantly more on concentrates ($54.79 vs $12.00) and vapes ($58.58 vs $38.88)

claim: 68.4% of Vermont cannabis consumer dollars are spent in the regulated market (dispensaries + delivery)
context: Percent of Dollars Spent in Regulated Market: Non-Patient 68.6%, Patient 67.5%, Total 68.4%.
notes: Remaining ~31.6% goes to friends/family purchases (18%), illicit sources (2-6%), homegrow (2%), free/gifted (1%), and other

claim: Vermont adult-use cannabis expenditure sources: Dispensary 69%, Purchased from friends/family 18%, Adult-use delivery/illicit 6%, Other 2%, Illicit 2%, Homegrow 2%, Free/gifted 1%
context: Cannabis Expenditures by Source (Adult Use): Dispensary (69%), Adult Use Delivery (Illicit - 6%), Purchased: Friends/Family (18%), Free/Gifted: Friends/Family (1%), Home grow (2%), Other (2%), Illicit (2%).
notes: Friends/family purchases represent a significant unregulated channel at 18%

claim: Vermont medical cannabis expenditure sources: Dispensary 65.1%, Purchased from friends/family 25%, Other 6.5%, Medical delivery 2.3%, Illicit 0.6%, Free/gifted 0.4%, Homegrow 0.1%
context: Cannabis Expenditures by Source (Medical): Dispensary (65.1%), Medical Delivery (2.3%), Free/Gifted: Friends/Family (0.4%), Home grow (0.1%), Purchased: Friends/Family (25%), Other (6.5%), Illicit (0.6%).
notes: Medical patients have higher friends/family purchasing (25% vs 18% adult-use) and lower dispensary share (65.1% vs 69%)

claim: Vermont cannabis monthly expenditures by product type and source, broken down by patient status
context: Monthly Expenditures Across Product & Source. Detailed breakdown of consumer spending by product category (flower, pre-rolls, edibles, beverages, concentrates, vapes, tinctures, topicals, capsules) and source (dispensary, free/gifted, purchased from friends/family, illicit, delivery, homegrow, other) for both patients and non-patients.

table_data.table_title: Monthly Expenditures Across Product & Source

notes: Flower dominates spending for both patients and non-patients. Patient concentrate spending is 4.6x non-patient. Vapes are the #2 category for patients.

claim: Top cannabis product characteristics valued by Vermont consumers: THC Potency (66%), Price (65%), Source (46%), Strain (45%), Convenience (43%)
context: Cannabis Product Characteristics (% Agreed): THC Potency 66.04%, Price 64.75%, Source 46.31%, Strain 45.21%, Convenience 42.68%, Tested 39.72%, Legal 28.08%, Terpene 19.81%, CBD Potency 8.3%, Delivery 1.71%, None of the Above 15.29%.
notes: Only 28% prioritize legality as a purchasing factor, suggesting price and potency drive purchasing decisions over regulatory compliance

claim: Washington legalized cannabis for recreational use in 2012 via voter initiative, tasking LCB with regulation
context: When Washingtonians authorized growing and selling cannabis for recreational purposes in 2012, they did so through an initiative that also promised tight control of a newly legal market.

claim: LCB employed about 370 people in 2024
context: The agency is led by a board appointed by the governor, and employed about 370 people in 2024.

claim: Washington applies a 37% excise tax on retail cannabis sales
context: Finally, state law made LCB responsible for collecting applicable excise taxes, currently a 37% excise tax.

claim: LCB collected almost $500 million in excise taxes in 2023
context: The agency collected almost $500 million in 2023. The resulting revenue is earmarked for a variety of purposes, including education, cannabis research and state health funds; a portion also contributes to the state general fund and LCB operations.
notes: Approximate figure; described as 'almost $500 million'

claim: LCB enforcement division has 28 commissioned law enforcement officers and 15 compliance consultants
context: For regulation of cannabis, the director of enforcement oversees 28 commissioned staff, who are law enforcement officers with authority to enforce some laws and regulations; and 15 compliance consultants, who offer licensees educational corrections.
notes: 28 commissioned staff + 15 compliance consultants = 43 total enforcement/education division field staff

claim: LCB finance division audit team has 2 supervisors and 11 staff members for revenue audits
context: Finance division audit team – This group, overseen by the chief financial officer, consists of two supervisors and 11 staff members. This team conducts revenue audits, including audits of cannabis licensees to confirm they have paid all necessary taxes.

claim: Washington's legal cannabis marketplace employs more than 100,000 people
context: Washington's legal cannabis marketplace is now an industry that employs more than 100,000 people, and the opportunities for illegal trade at any point in the process remain real.
notes: This figure from the audit report appears to include broader supply chain employment; the Licensing Analysis report cites 18,700 direct workers

claim: LEAF cannabis tracking system launched February 2018 but ultimately failed due to project management and vendor issues
context: The agency launched LEAF in 2018, but it ultimately failed due to project management and vendor issues. After deeming LEAF too unreliable, LCB deployed a stopgap data reporting system known as CCRS (the Cannabis Central Reporting System) in 2021, which had its own significant limitations.

claim: LCB does not expect to fully implement a replacement cannabis tracking system until 2031, nearly 20 years after legalization
context: With the present audit, however, we discovered that the cannabis tracking system subsequently failed. Facing the need to replace other aging information technology systems, LCB does not expect to fully implement a more efficient tracking system until 2031 – nearly 20 years after the legal recreational cannabis market was authorized by voters.

claim: Between July 2018 and July 2023, LCB saw turnover in half a dozen executive positions responsible for software projects
context: Between July 2018 and July 2023, LCB saw turnover in a half dozen executive positions, including people in roles that were responsible for LEAF, the tracking software that replaced BioTrack. As of July 2024, nearly all leadership at LCB is new within the last three years.

claim: CCRS contained roughly 96 million records for sales, product/plant information, and plant destructions
context: We also reviewed data reported to LCB over several years, which included a total of roughly 96 million records for sales, product and plant information, and plant destructions.

claim: Analysis of about 70 million sales records identified about 1.6 million instances of negative amounts and 2.5 million instances of null or zero values
context: Based on our analysis of about 70 million sales records, reported from July 2022 through August 7, 2023, we identified about 1.6 million instances in which licensees reported negative amounts for this information, and about 2.5 million instances where a null or zero-value was reported.
notes: 1.6 million negative amounts + 2.5 million null/zero values = 4.1 million total problematic entries out of ~70 million records

claim: 2022 annual reported sales in CCRS were almost $8 billion due to misplaced decimals, while Department of Revenue estimated actual sales at about $1.3 billion
context: LCB managers also described an extensive problem in reported sales information, involving misplaced decimals in the reported sales price of individual products. As a result, reported sales in CCRS were greatly overstated for a period of time, most noticeably in 2022. 2022 annual reported sales in CCRS were almost $8 billion. The Department of Revenue, however, estimated sales for the same time period to be about $1.3 billion.
notes: CCRS reported $7.70 billion vs. actual ~$1.3 billion due to decimal errors. Department of Revenue estimate includes non-cannabis sales like paraphernalia.

claim: CCRS product, plant and destruction data included almost 60,000 products with reported dates from 1899-2003 and about 600,000 records with future dates
context: Our analysis of product, plant and plant destruction data reported to CCRS through August 7, 2023 (about 25 million records), included numerous records reflecting dates from before cannabis was legalized, including almost 60,000 products with reported dates ranging from 1899-2003. We also identified about 600,000 reported dates that were in the future, including for harvests and plant destructions.
notes: ~60,000 records with historical dates (1899-2003) + ~600,000 future-dated records out of ~25 million total records

claim: In 2023, lab testing found unacceptable levels of DDT-related chemicals in cannabis on the market, and LCB struggled to trace affected products due to CCRS limitations
context: In 2023, lab testing found unacceptable levels of chemicals related to DDT, a synthetic pesticide banned half a century ago, in cannabis on the market for consumption. LCB data staff were able to develop reports using dates of sale matched with likely processing time that eventually led them to the likely plants of origin, but it took longer than leadership wanted. The agency resorted to manual methods to fully track down all the tainted product and remove it from the market, and still had no way to guarantee all the affected products had been recalled.

claim: LCB systems modernization project will replace 40-year-old core systems used for licensing and enforcement
context: The systems modernization project is no small undertaking: most LCB IT personnel and other key employees are involved in its delivery. The project will replace 40-year-old systems used for core agency functions, such as licensing and enforcement, which are nearing the end of their life cycles.

claim: In 2022 and 2023, average annual turnover for cannabis enforcement officers was about 50%
context: In 2022 and 2023, average annual turnover for cannabis enforcement officers was about 50%. At the same time, LCB said it had extensive vacancies; for example, on November 1, 2023, about one-third of the 26 enforcement officer positions were vacant.

claim: On November 1, 2023, about one-third of the 26 enforcement officer positions were vacant
context: At the same time, LCB said it had extensive vacancies; for example, on November 1, 2023, about one-third of the 26 enforcement officer positions were vacant. The agency filled all but four positions by April 1, 2024.
notes: Approximately 8-9 of 26 positions vacant

claim: LCB conducted premises checks at 89% of licensees in 2023, falling short of its 100% goal
context: LCB did not meet some of its internal performance goals related to enforcement processes in 2023, which were to conduct premises checks at 100% of locations and close 90% of complaints within 60 days. LCB reported that it conducted premises checks at 89% of licensees; staff said it closed only 59% of complaints within 60 days.
notes: Goal was 100%

claim: LCB closed only 59% of complaints within 60 days in 2023, far below its 90% goal
context: LCB did not meet some of its internal performance goals related to enforcement processes in 2023, which were to conduct premises checks at 100% of locations and close 90% of complaints within 60 days. LCB reported that it conducted premises checks at 89% of licensees; staff said it closed only 59% of complaints within 60 days.
notes: Goal was 90%

claim: LCB seized cannabis products at 39 locations in 2023 and 38 locations in 2024 as part of diversion and inversion enforcement
context: Prioritization of diversion and inversion is clear in the seizing of cannabis products at 39 locations in 2023 and 38 locations in 2024 thus far.
notes: 2024 figure is partial year (through October 2024)

claim: Youth access compliance rate for cannabis is consistently above 95%
context: Preventing youth access to cannabis is a key focus, and the compliance rate is consistently above 95%.
notes: Per LCB agency response to audit

claim: LCB cannabis tracking system timeline: BioTrack (2014-2017), no system/Excel (Nov 2017-Jan 2018), LEAF (Feb 2018-Dec 2021), CCRS (Dec 2021-present), replacement predicted 2031
context: Timeline of Cannabis Data Tracking Systems: BioTrack 2014-2017; No data system (Excel files) Nov. 2017 - Jan. 2018; LEAF Feb. 2018 - Dec. 2021; Cannabis Central Reporting System (CCRS) Dec. 2021 - Present; Predicted replacement for CCRS: 2031.

table_data.table_title: Timeline of Cannabis Data Tracking Systems

claim: Washington retailers sold $1.22 billion in cannabis products in 2023
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: There are 473 cannabis retail licenses issued by LCB. In 2023, Washington retailers sold $1.22 billion in cannabis products.

claim: There are 473 cannabis retail licenses issued by LCB as of April 2024
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: There are 473 cannabis retail licenses issued by LCB. In 2023, Washington retailers sold $1.22 billion in cannabis products.

claim: By 2030-2035, 634 retail licenses across Washington are estimated to be economically viable
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: By 2030-2035, 634 retail licenses across Washington are estimated to be economically viable.
notes: Based on Threshold of Economic Viability (TEV) of $2.5 million per licensee with 10 employees

claim: Between 50%-55% legal consumer participation in Washington cannabis market, with 45%-47% of total demand satisfied via illicit channels
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: There was between 50% - 55% legal consumer participation. Described differently, between 45% - 47% of the total demand was satisfied via illicit channels. This is a lower number than previous reports provided to the LCB due to differences in the methodological approaches and the number of consumers in the market.
notes: Midpoint of 50-55% range used for value

claim: Maximum theoretical potential of 793 retail licenses if 100% legal participation (zero illicit demand)
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: If there was 100% legal participation by consumers (zero illicit demand) there would be a maximum potential of 793 retail licenses at any given time. This is considered the theoretical maximum in terms of licensure. Most states achieve between 80%-85% legal participation once their markets have matured.

claim: In 2034-2035, estimated demand for 891 thousand square feet of cultivation that can be allocated to various producers
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: In 2034-2035, there is an estimated demand for 891 thousand square feet that can be allocated to various producers depending on license type. The total market demand is the total amount of cultivated output required to support all cannabis product types Flower, pre-rolls, edibles, tinctures, etc.

claim: Current total supply capacity is 2.6 million pounds; 2024 total supply requirements are 612 thousand pounds, meaning producers need less than 30% capacity
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: Current total supply capacity is 2.6 million pounds. In 2024, total supply requirements (Cultivated output) are 612 thousand pounds. This means that all supply required to satisfy the legal demand would only require producers to operate at less than 30% capacity.

claim: In 2034-2035, 1,782 to 3,340 processor licenses in Washington are estimated to be economically viable
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: In 2034-2035, 1,782 to 3,340 processor licenses in Washington are estimated to be economically viable. This includes such businesses as edible manufacturers, tinctures, balms, vapes and other non-flower oriented 'derivative' products.
notes: Range depends on assumption of 500 lbs vs 250 lbs of cultivated supply per license

claim: Projected cannabis license demand forecast: 5-year (2029) and 10-year (2034) by type
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: The below chart shows the estimated forecasted demand for cannabis licenses in Washington. For reference, there are currently 473 retail licenses, 1,039 processor licenses (with most attached to a producer license as well), and a total supply capacity of 2.6 million pounds from producers.

table_data.table_title: Projected Number of Cannabis Licenses To Be Economically Viable

notes: Producer demand in pounds of output. Processor forecast assumes 500 lbs per license. Retail forecast assumes TEV of $2.5 million per licensee.

claim: U.S. total addressable cannabis market demand is $105 billion, with only $28.8 billion satisfied through legal channels in 2023
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: There are billions of dollars of demand for cannabis in the United States, and yet only a minority of the demand is satisfied via legal, regulated markets. Of the $105 billion in U.S. Total Market Demand (TAM) only $28.8 billion was satisfied through legal channels that are regulated by the states.
notes: National figure for context

claim: Total legal U.S. cannabis retail sales were $28.8 billion in 2023, averaging $2.4 billion per month, up from $26.1 billion the year before
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: The total legal revenues associated with cannabis sales in the U.S. totaled $28.8 billion in 2023. Legal U.S. cannabis retail sales were an average of $2.4 billion per month. This was an increase from $26.1 billion the year before.
notes: National figure for context

claim: Only 24% of cannabis operators nationally reported being profitable
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: In a recent survey on business conditions, cannabis operators reported that only 24% of operators nationally were profitable. While inputs from Washington on the survey were limited, this trend appears to also hold true for Washington operators.
notes: National figure; trend appears to hold for WA

claim: Washington has issued approximately 2,498 active cannabis licenses for producers, processors, retailers and distributors
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: Washington has issued approximately 2,498 active cannabis licenses for producers, processors, retailers and distributors. These licenses represent more than 4% of all U.S. cannabis licenses.

claim: Washington employs more than 18,700 cannabis workers, out of 440,445 workers in the U.S. legal cannabis industry
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: Washington also employs more than 18,700 workers, out of the 440,445 workers in the U.S. legal cannabis industry. With over $1.2 billion in sales, and such strong employment, the Washington cannabis market is a strong driver of economic activity in the state.

claim: Washington cannabis revenue is roughly split between flower sales (47.5%) and derivative product sales (52.5%)
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: From a revenue perspective, the Washington market is roughly split between flower sales (47.5%) and derivative product sales (52.5%).
notes: Derivative products include concentrates (38.63%), edibles (12.54%), others (1.34%)

claim: Washington ranks 14th overall in total addressable market, 9th in total consumers, and 10th in total legal sales nationally
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: Washington ranks 14th overall in terms of total addressable market (This number is based on the number of consumers in the state). National statistics rank Washington 9th overall in the number of total consumers based in 2022. At $1.2 billion in sales in 2023, Washington ranked 10th in total legal sales.

claim: Consumers will drive up to 30 minutes to purchase cannabis products legally; beyond that, legal participation declines
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: Consumer access helps to reduce the influence of the illicit market and helps drive legal participation. Consumers will drive up to 30 minutes to purchase cannabis products legally, however if they have to travel too far, legal participation declines.

claim: In states with access to legal cannabis, opioid prescriptions decline 11% and opioid-related deaths decline 24.8% (NIH study)
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: In a study from the National Institute of Health (NIH), in states where there is access to legal cannabis, prescriptions for opioids decline 11% and opioid related deaths decline 24.8%.
notes: National NIH research cited in WA report context

claim: AMA research found passage of recreational cannabis laws was not associated with increased adolescent cannabis use; each additional year of legalization associated with 8% higher odds of zero cannabis use among youth
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: In a report published by the American Medical Association (AMA), access to legal cannabis results in a decrease in youth usage. The report indicated that passage of recreational cannabis laws (RCL) 'was not associated with adolescents' likelihood or frequency of cannabis use.' 'Each additional year of RCL,' the study says, 'was associated with 8% higher odds of zero cannabis use (lower likelihood of any use).'
notes: National AMA research cited in WA report context

claim: U.S. retail cannabis revenues account for roughly 59.3% of the entire cannabis value chain
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: Overall, the revenues in the retail sector account for roughly 59.3% of the entire U.S. cannabis value chain.
notes: National figure for context

claim: U.S. cannabis retailers averaged $2.5 million per year in revenue; Washington retailers averaged $2.6 million, ranking 23rd nationally
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: U.S. retailers averaged $2.5 million per year in revenue. Washington retailers averaged $2.6 million ranking 23rd out of the 38 states with some form of legal access. It is somewhat coincidental that Washington retail averages mirror the U.S. national average for retail sales per store.

claim: Cannabis operators experience effective federal tax rate in excess of 70% due to 280E; the overall industry paid over $2.12 billion in federal taxes in 2022, of which $1.81 billion was paid by retailers
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: Due to the federal illegality of cannabis, cannabis operators are not permitted to claim many deductions off of their federal tax liability. As a result, cannabis retailers often experience an effective tax rate in excess of 70%. In a recent study conducted by Whitney Economics, the overall cannabis industry paid in excess of $2.12 billion in federal taxes in 2022 as a result of the federal tax code 280E limiting standard business deductions. Of this amount, $1.81 billion was paid by retailers.
notes: National figure. 280E tax code limits standard business deductions.

claim: Cannabis business loans carry interest rates in the range of 25%-35%
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: Typical financial instruments are not available to cannabis operators, since banks are reluctant to make loans. The loans that are made are often high interest loans, with rates in the range of 25% - 35%.
notes: Range of 25-35%

claim: A cannabis retailer needs to generate approximately $2.5 million per year (TEV) to remain viable; smaller rural operators require approximately $1.6 million
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: Considering the cost structure of a cannabis retailer, the highest costs to run the business are product acquisition, labor and federal taxes. In order to cover additional costs of doing business such as rent, health care, security, compliance, etc. A retailer needs to generate approximately $2.5 million per year in order to remain viable. Smaller operators in rural parts of the U.S. require approximately $1.6 million per year as they require less labor.
notes: TEV = Threshold of Economic Viability

claim: King County has highest TEV at $4.2 million/year, while Ferry County has lowest at $2.16 million/year
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: This chart examines some of the main components of determining the threshold of economic viability by county. It examines sales, product acquisition costs, labor, federal taxes, additional costs (Rent, Debt Service, Insurance, Compliance, etc). The model also incorporates roughly $100,000 of additional expenses that are required throughout the year.

table_data.table_title: Determinants in the Threshold of Economic Viability (Selected Counties)

notes: TEV varies by county based on labor costs. Full table has all 39 WA counties.

claim: Washington has 473 current retail licenses with county-level distribution; King County has the most at 104
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: This chart lists the number of licenses issued for retail in Washington as of 04/03/2024. While there are additional licenses allocated and some that are banned in counties, this chart is intended to show how many licenses that are currently active by the LCB at the county level.

table_data.table_title: Total Current Retail Licenses by County

notes: Top 11 counties shown. Full table in report has all 39 counties.

claim: Maximum potential retail licenses is 793 at 100% legal participation; most states achieve 80%-85% legal participation
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: This chart lists the total maximum number of retail licenses by county assuming all cannabis demand is satisfied via legal channels. Any additional license issuance would be greater than the demand in the market. Washington 793 Max Potential. From a theoretical perspective, 793 is the maximum number of retail licenses that the state of Washington could support assuming all sales were made from the legal market. Given most states achieve between 80%-85% legal participation, retail licenses issued would be less than this amount and would cap out at roughly 630 - 650 retail licenses.

table_data.table_title: Maximum Number of Retail Licenses by County (Top Counties)

claim: 49.1% of Washington general public felt number of cannabis retail outlets was 'about right'; 25.0% thought there were too many
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: Based on a survey deployed in 2022, a majority (49.1%) of the general public in Washington felt that the number of cannabis retail outlets was 'about right', while 25.0% of survey respondents thought there were already too many.

table_data.table_title: Consumer Perception of Retail Saturation (University of Waterloo 2022)

notes: Source: University of Waterloo - Washington 2022 Cannabis Report (May 2023)

claim: Forecasted retail license demand: by 2025 demand exceeds current 473 licenses; by 2030 demand reaches 634
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: This chart examines the current total number of licenses issued in Washington and then compares that to the total number that are forecasted in the future. The forecast is based on the level of legal participation that is anticipated for each year through 2035.

table_data.table_title: Current Active Licenses vs. Future Demand

notes: Negative means more viable licenses are needed than currently issued

claim: There are 986 active producer and producer-processor licenses in Washington with total capacity of 2.61 million pounds
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: There are currently 986 active producer and producer processor licenses in Washington. The total capacity associated with this level of licensure is 2.61 million pounds of cultivated output.

claim: Total demand assuming 100% legal market is 1.11 million pounds; excess supply capacity is 1.50 million pounds
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: The total demand, assuming that all 100% of the market is satisfied via legal channels, is 1.11 million pounds. Therefore, there is 1.50 million pounds of excess capacity assuming full capacity utilization. The entire market (illicit and legal) can be supported with less than 45% of current capacity utilization.
notes: Current licensed capacity of 2,610,639 lbs vs. max demand of 1,113,484 lbs = 1,497,155 lbs excess

claim: Cannabis cultivation output per square foot: Indoor 0.432 lbs, Greenhouse 0.217 lbs, Outdoor 0.101 lbs
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: Each grow type (Indoor, Outdoor or Greenhouse (Both)) has different output for each square foot of canopy. The estimates in this chart are provided by Resource Innovation Institute (RII). RII tracks output per square foot for producers across the country in their 'Power Score' tool.

table_data.table_title: Output per Square Foot by Grow Type

notes: Source: Resource Innovation Institute

claim: LCB reported in 2022 that producers were only utilizing roughly 50% of their capacity
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: This actually shows the extent of the potential overage. Given the fact that not all of the demand is supplied via legal channels, the current annual legal demand could be supported through 2035 with a capacity utilization of 30% - 35%. Based on previous LCB statements in 2022, cultivators ran on the average at 50% capacity utilization.

claim: 84.2% of cannabis producers in Washington are also cannabis processors
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: In a period of rising input costs (Labor, electricity, interest rates, etc.), producers may struggle with profitability. This is one reason why a majority of the producers (84.2%) are also cannabis processors.

claim: Supply demand forecast by year: 2024 demand is 612,416 lbs, rising to 890,787 lbs by 2030-2035
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: This chart examines the amount of supply in pounds that is forecasted to meet the legal demand in the state. It then examines how much capacity needs to be utilized in order to meet that demand.

table_data.table_title: Supply Output Scenarios vs. Forecasted Demand

notes: 30% utilization covers demand through 2027; 40% utilization covers demand through 2035

claim: There are 1,039 active processor licenses in Washington
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: The number of licenses currently issued and active is 1,039. Because it takes so little raw material to generate enough oil to make viable, sellable retail products, licenses in this section are based on the total amount of the potential available supply.

claim: Retail sales breakdown: Flower/Pre-Rolls 47.49%, Derivative Products 52.51% (Concentrates 38.63%, Edibles 12.54%, Others 1.34%)
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: This chart examines the percentage of sales in Washington that was flower or biomass versus derivative products (Edibles, concentrates, tinctures, etc) It shows that derivative products make up the majority of the retail sales in Washington.

table_data.table_title: Legal Sales Breakdown: Flower vs. Derivatives

notes: Source: LCB

claim: Maximum potential for processor licenses: 4,454 at 250 lbs/license, 2,227 at 500 lbs/license; current is 1,039
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: Regardless of which model is used, there is a max potential of between 1,188 and 3,415 additional licenses that can be issued.

table_data.table_title: Processing Licenses Issued vs. Max Potential

source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: This chart examines the opportunities for additional licenses based on the forecasted demand for licenses from 2024-2035. A red number in parenthesis indicated an opportunity to issue more licenses.

table_data.table_title: Processor License Forecast (500 lb/license model)

notes: Negative gap means opportunity to issue more licenses

claim: Forecasted processor license demand (250 lb model): 2,450 in 2024 growing to 3,563 by 2030-2035
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: This chart examines the opportunities for additional licenses based on the forecasted demand for licenses from 2024-2035. A red number in parenthesis indicated an opportunity to issue more licenses.

table_data.table_title: Processor License Forecast (250 lb/license model)
