claim: Cannabis flower and pre-rolled joint demand make up between 45%-50% of total retail sales revenue while derivative products make up the rest
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: Cannabis flower and pre-rolled 'joint' demand make up between 45% - 50% of the total retail sales revenue, while derivative products such as vaporizers, edibles and tinctures make up the rest.
notes: Midpoint of 45-50% range used for value

claim: Washington cannabis demand elasticity estimated at -2.1 to -2.4, where any number greater than -1.0 is considered elastic
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: Whitney Economics has set the industry standard for cannabis demand elasticity at between -2.1 to -2.4, where any number greater than -1.0 is considered elastic. As a result, the key to consumer participation is keeping prices low (relative to the illicit channel) and providing consumers with access.
notes: Midpoint of -2.1 to -2.4 range. Highly elastic demand means consumers are very price-sensitive.

claim: Higher prices (18%) and less convenience (18%) were top reasons consumers did not buy cannabis legally in 2021
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: Higher prices and less convenience led the list of reasons buyers said they chose to buy cannabis illegally last year, with 18% of respondents citing each of those categories.
notes: Source: University of Waterloo - Washington 2022 Cannabis Report

claim: Washington licenses represent more than 4% of all U.S. cannabis licenses
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: Washington has issued approximately 2,498 active cannabis licenses for producers, processors, retailers and distributors. These licenses represent more than 4% of all U.S. cannabis licenses.

claim: Report recommends not issuing additional producer licenses until supply capacity is more in line with demand
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: Based on the analysis of the current and future demand and supply in Washington, here is a series of recommendations: 1) Do not issue additional producer licenses until the supply capacity is more in line with the demand that is based on legal consumer participation.

claim: Report recommends issuing retail licenses in underserved counties sooner rather than later; licensing process takes 1-2 years
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: Issue retail licenses in underserved counties sooner rather than later assuming there is no moratorium. If there is a moratorium, then prepare for license issuance in case the ban is lifted. Either way, it will take time to identify locations, receive funding and acquire permits. Since this can take years and the number of licenses in demand is increasing, issuing licenses so that they are available when demanded is a disciplined approach.

claim: Product manufacturing/processing is where the greatest economic opportunities exist for future applicants
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: Product manufacturing is where the greatest economic opportunities are for future applicants.

claim: 40 U.S. states have deployed medical cannabis regulatory programs, 24 of which are also adult-use
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: A lot has changed since 2012 and now 40 states have deployed a medical cannabis regulatory program of which 24 are also adult-use.
notes: National context

claim: U.S. legal cannabis industry employs 440,445 workers total
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: Washington also employs more than 18,700 workers, out of the 440,445 workers in the U.S. legal cannabis industry.
notes: National figure for context

claim: There are 569 total licenses allocated by LCB for retail, but not all will be needed until the 2027/2028 timeframe
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: There are 569 licenses allocated by the LCB, but not all will be needed until the 2027/2028 timeframe. If greater legal participation is achieved, there will be an increased demand for additional retail outlets sooner.
notes: 473 are active/issued; 569 are total allocated

claim: Washington is considered a mature state cannabis market
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: States in our national analysis are broken out into three categories: 1) Mature states, 2) Ramping state markets, and 3) Emerging state markets. Mature state markets have deployed legal programs and have converted a majority of the total potential demand into the legal marketplace. The state of Washington would be considered a mature state market.

claim: Current total supply capacity could meet entire market demand (illicit + legal) with less than 45% capacity utilization
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: The entire market (illicit and legal) can be supported with less than 45% of current capacity utilization.

claim: Derivative products account for 50%-55% of all retail revenues with a demographic shift in consumer demand favoring derivatives like beverages
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: There is a demographic shift in consumer demand in favor of derivative products (such as beverages), with 50% and 55% of all retail revenues coming from derivative products.

claim: Okanogan County has the most active producer licenses at 112; Grant County has 88; Snohomish has 82
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: Supply vs. Demand chart listing active producer licenses and total supply capacity by county.

table_data.table_title: Top Counties by Active Producer Licenses

notes: Top 7 counties by number of producer licenses shown

claim: King County has largest demand at 185,507 lbs in 2024 but only 104,242 lbs supply capacity from 44 producers, indicating deficit
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: Supply vs. Demand chart: King County has 44 active producer licenses with 104,242 lbs total supply capacity and demand of 185,507 lbs in 2024.
notes: King County demand exceeds local supply capacity; other counties supply the difference

claim: Washington took a limited licensing approach for retail but kept cultivation and processing licenses unlimited
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: Washington took a different approach to cannabis licensing, limiting the number of retail licenses (The application window was open for only a limited amount of time after both I-502 in 2012 and SB-5052 in 2021-2022 and has not been re-opened), while largely keeping cultivation and processing licenses unlimited.

claim: 2024 legal supply demand is 612,416 pounds; this can be met with 30% capacity utilization of existing licensed capacity
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: 2024 Supply Forecast based on Demand: 612,416 pounds. At 30% utilization, licensed capacity produces 783,192 pounds, which covers 2024 demand.

claim: In 2022, Washington legislature passed RCW 69.50.335 and RCW 69.50.345 tasking LCB with developing county-level license thresholds
source_report: State Reports/WA State Licensing Analysis Report May 17 2024.pdf
context: The Washington legislature passed RCW 69.50.335 and RCW 69.50.345 into law and the LCB was tasked with developing thresholds for licensure and to focus these thresholds at the county level.

claim: Whitney Economics estimated 634 retail licenses may be economically viable in Washington by 2032, approximately 160 more than the current 473
source_report: State Reports/Whitney Economics Report Key Takeaways Aug 19 2024.pdf
context: Retail opportunities are considered available but limited in scope. It was estimated that 634 retail licenses may be economically viable in 2032, suggesting that ~160 more retail licenses could be viable in eight years.
notes: Independent report by Whitney Economics for WA LCB. Does not represent official LCB position.

claim: Washington has the capacity to produce approximately 2.6 million pounds of cannabis biomass annually, but estimated 2032 demand is only 891 thousand pounds
source_report: State Reports/Whitney Economics Report Key Takeaways Aug 19 2024.pdf
context: Producers have the capacity to supply more cannabis than demand. It is estimated that Washington has the ability to produce ~2.6 million pounds of biomass each year. By 2032, there will be an estimated demand of 891 thousand pounds. This calculation suggests producers cannot all operate at full scale given the total output capacity.
notes: Massive oversupply: capacity is ~2.9x projected 2032 demand

claim: Estimated 2032 demand for cannabis biomass in Washington is 891,000 pounds
source_report: State Reports/Whitney Economics Report Key Takeaways Aug 19 2024.pdf
context: Producers have the capacity to supply more cannabis than demand. It is estimated that Washington has the ability to produce ~2.6 million pounds of biomass each year. By 2032, there will be an estimated demand of 891 thousand pounds. This calculation suggests producers cannot all operate at full scale given the total output capacity.

claim: Whitney Economics estimates demand for between 1,782 and 3,340 processor licenses in Washington by 2032, at least 740 more than current
source_report: State Reports/Whitney Economics Report Key Takeaways Aug 19 2024.pdf
context: Processor licenses are considered to be the largest opportunity in the cannabis market. Whitney Economics estimates there will be demand for between 1,782 and 3,340 processor licenses in 2032, suggesting that at least 740 more processer licenses could be viable in eight years.
notes: Range: 1,782 (lower) to 3,340 (higher). Current count is 1,039.

claim: Washington had 473 retail cannabis licenses as of May 2024
source_report: State Reports/Whitney Economics Report Key Takeaways Aug 19 2024.pdf
context: Table 1. Retail License Analysis showing current (May 2024), Recommended Estimate 2032, and High Estimate 2032 for all Washington counties.

However, there was an additional calculation (titled 'High Estimate') based on 5 retail employees because of the recognition that some stores may be smaller than others.

claim: King County has the largest projected demand for both retail and processor licenses in Washington by 2032
source_report: State Reports/Whitney Economics Report Key Takeaways Aug 19 2024.pdf
context: King County: Current retail 104, recommended 2032 estimate 134, high estimate 313. Current processor 82, lower estimate 540, higher estimate 1,079. King County also has the highest estimated biomass demand at 269,828 pounds in 2032.
notes: King County (Seattle metro) represents ~30% of projected processor demand and ~29% of projected retail demand under high estimate
